perturbation.original_text: This Non-Disclosure Agreement (the "Agreement") is entered into by and between __________________________________________________ located at ____________________________________________________________________________________ _____________________________________________________________, (the "Disclosing Party") and __________________________________________________ located at ____________________________________________________________________________________ _______________________________________________________________________, (the "Receiving Party") on ________ day of ______________, _________ for the purpose of preventing the unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information as defined below. The parties agree to enter into a confidential relationship concerning the disclosure of certain proprietary and confidential information ("Confidential Information").
perturbation.changed_text: This Non-Disclosure Agreement (the "Agreement") is entered into by and between __________________________________________________ located at ____________________________________________________________________________________ _____________________________________________________________, (the "Data Provider") and __________________________________________________ located at ____________________________________________________________________________________ _______________________________________________________________________, (the "Receiving Party") on ________ day of ______________, _________ for the purpose of preventing the unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information as defined below. The parties agree to enter into a confidential relationship concerning the disclosure of certain proprietary and confidential information ("Confidential Information").
perturbation.explanation: The term 'Disclosing Party' is changed to 'Data Provider'. While the agreement later uses the term 'Disclosing Party', this initial change causes confusion as to which term is the legally binding one, leading to a contradiction. The receiving party is unsure of whom to work with. This introduces uncertainty in enforcement and creates an in-text contradiction, making it unclear who is responsible for disclosing information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1. Confidential Information For purposes of this Agreement, "Confidential Information" shall include all information or material that has or could have commercial value or other utility in the business in which Disclosing Party is engaged. If Confidential Information is in written form, the Disclosing Party shall label or stamp the materials with the word "Confidential" or some similar warning. If Confidential Information is transmitted orally, the Disclosing Party shall promptly provide writing indicating that such oral communication constitutes Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text uses 'Disclosing Party' and the changed text replaces it with 'Data Provider'. The contradicted text references 'Disclosing Party'. This creates a contradiction because the agreement now uses two different terms for the same entity without defining them interchangeably, leading to uncertainty about which entity is responsible for labeling and providing written confirmation of Confidential Information, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party's obligations under this Agreement do not extend to information that is: (a) publicly known at the time of disclosure or subsequently becomes publicly known through no fault of the Receiving Party; (b) discovered or created by the Receiving Party before disclosure by Disclosing Party; (c) learned by the Receiving Party through legitimate means other than from the Disclosing Party or Disclosing Party's representatives; or (d) is disclosed by Receiving Party with Disclosing Party's prior written approval.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party's obligations under this Agreement do not extend to information that is: (a) publicly known at the time of disclosure or subsequently becomes publicly known through no fault of the Receiving Party; (b) discovered or created by the Receiving Party before disclosure by Data Originator; (c) learned by the Receiving Party through legitimate means other than from the Data Originator or Data Originator's representatives; or (d) is disclosed by Receiving Party with Data Originator's prior written approval.
perturbation.explanation: Here, I am again replacing 'Disclosing Party' with 'Data Originator'. This creates a conflict because the agreement consistently uses 'Disclosing Party' elsewhere. Now, Section 2 refers to a 'Data Originator,' while other sections still reference the 'Disclosing Party'. This raises questions about whether these terms are interchangeable and about the scope of the Receiving Party's obligations with different parties. Also, 'Data Provider' was used in the first perturbation, and now 'Data Originator', making the terminology for a same role inconsistent.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. Obligations of Receiving Party 3.1 Receiving Party shall hold and maintain the Confidential Information in strictest confidence for the sole and exclusive benefit of the Disclosing Party. 3.2 Receiving Party shall carefully restrict access to Confidential Information to employees, contractors and third parties as is reasonably required and shall require those persons to sign non-disclosure restrictions at least as protective as those in this Agreement. 3.3 Receiving Party shall not, without the prior written approval of Disclosing Party, use for Receiving Party's benefit, publish, copy, or otherwise disclose to others, or permit the use by others for their benefit or to the detriment of Disclosing Party, any Confidential Information. 3.4 Receiving Party shall return to Disclosing Party any and all records, notes, and other written, printed, or tangible materials in its possession pertaining to Confidential Information immediately if Disclosing Party requests it in writing.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'Disclosing Party' with 'Data Originator'. The contradicted text repeatedly refers to 'Disclosing Party'. Since the terms are not defined as interchangeable, and 'Data Provider' was previously introduced, this creates a strong inconsistency and ambiguity regarding which party holds the rights and responsibilities outlined in Section 3, causing an in-text contradiction as described by the 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall hold and maintain the Confidential Information in strictest confidence for the sole and exclusive benefit of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall hold and maintain the Confidential Information in strictest confidence for the sole and exclusive benefit of the Information Source.
perturbation.explanation: The original text refers to 'Disclosing Party', whereas the changed text now introduces 'Information Source', creating ambiguity. This creates uncertainty about which party the Receiving Party owes the duty of confidentiality, especially when combined with prior perturbations. This creates a direct contradiction in the context of the entire agreement, since 'Information Source' and 'Disclosing Party' are used as a the same legal entity. As, the terminology used, in different sections, is not the same.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.3 Receiving Party shall not, without the prior written approval of Disclosing Party, use for Receiving Party's benefit, publish, copy, or otherwise disclose to others, or permit the use by others for their benefit or to the detriment of Disclosing Party, any Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The change replaces 'Disclosing Party' with 'Information Source'. The contradicted text explicitly states 'Disclosing Party'. This creates a direct contradiction, as the Receiving Party's obligations are now unclear: should they seek approval from the 'Disclosing Party' or the 'Information Source' before using or disclosing the Confidential Information? This misalignment of terminology, as defined in the instructions, leads to an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall return to Disclosing Party any and all records, notes, and other written, printed, or tangible materials in its possession pertaining to Confidential Information immediately if Disclosing Party requests it in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall return to Data Controller any and all records, notes, and other written, printed, or tangible materials in its possession pertaining to Confidential Information immediately if Data Controller requests it in writing.
perturbation.explanation: Here, 'Disclosing Party' is replaced with 'Data Controller.' This introduces a contradiction because the document still extensively refers to 'Disclosing Party,' 'Information Source,' and 'Data Originator' from previous changes. It makes the requirement of returning the data unclear, as it's uncertain who the 'Data Controller' is in relation to the 'Disclosing Party.' Now Section 3.4 states something very different from the title 'Obligations of Receiving Party'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. Time Periods The non-disclosure provisions of this Agreement shall survive the termination of this Agreement and Receiving Party's duty to hold Confidential Information in confidence shall remain in effect until the Confidential Information no longer qualifies as a trade secret or until Disclosing Party sends Receiving Party written notice releasing Receiving Party from this Agreement, whichever occurs first.
perturbation.justification: The change replaces 'Disclosing Party' with 'Data Controller'. The contradicted text refers to the 'Disclosing Party' in the context of the agreement's survivability and the confidentiality obligations. Replacing 'Disclosing Party' with 'Data Controller' creates a contradiction because it is unclear who can release the Receiving Party from the agreement. This creates a strong contradiction because the change alters who can perform an action, potentially making the section unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: The non-disclosure provisions of this Agreement shall survive the termination of this Agreement and Receiving Party's duty to hold Confidential Information in confidence shall remain in effect until the Confidential Information no longer qualifies as a trade secret or until Disclosing Party sends Receiving Party written notice releasing Receiving Party from this Agreement, whichever occurs first.
perturbation.changed_text: The non-disclosure provisions of this Agreement shall survive the termination of this Agreement and Receiving Party's duty to hold Confidential Information in confidence shall remain in effect until the Confidential Information no longer qualifies as a trade secret or until the Original Disclosing Party sends Receiving Party written notice releasing Receiving Party from this Agreement, whichever occurs first.
perturbation.explanation: Changing `Disclosing Party` to `Original Disclosing Party` introduces ambiguity. The contract does not clarify what differentiates the `Original Disclosing Party` from just the `Disclosing Party`. This lack of clarity undermines the clause's enforceability, as it's unclear which party must provide the written notice, leading to an in-text contradiction. Is `Original Disclosing Party` a subset of `Disclosing Party`?
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7. Notice of Breach The Receiving Party shall immediately notify the Disclosing Party upon discovering any unauthorized use of disclosure of Confidential Information by the Receiving Party or its Representatives, or any other breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or its Representatives, and will cooperate with any efforts by the DIsclosing Party to assist the Disclosing Party to regain the possession of its Confidential Information and thus prevent its further unauthorized use.
perturbation.justification: The change from `Disclosing Party` to `Original Disclosing Party` introduces ambiguity because the contract doesn't define `Original Disclosing Party`, creating uncertainty about which entity can release the Receiving Party from the agreement. This contradicts the contradicted text, which refers to `Disclosing Party` in the context of notification of breach, leading to confusion about which party the Receiving Party must notify.

perturbation.original_text: Notice of Breach The Receiving Party shall immediately notify the Disclosing Party upon discovering any unauthorized use of disclosure of Confidential Information by the Receiving Party or its Representatives, or any other breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or its Representatives, and will cooperate with any efforts by the DIsclosing Party to assist the Disclosing Party to regain the possession of its Confidential Information and thus prevent its further unauthorized use.
perturbation.changed_text: Notice of Breach The Receiving Party shall immediately notify the Data Fiduciary upon discovering any unauthorized use of disclosure of Confidential Information by the Receiving Party or its Representatives, or any other breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or its Representatives, and will cooperate with any efforts by the Data Fiduciary to assist the Data Fiduciary to regain the possession of its Confidential Information and thus prevent its further unauthorized use.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "Disclosing Party" with "Data Fiduciary" creates confusion as to whom the Receiving Party should report breaches. Since the document uses "Data Provider", "Data Originator", "Information Source", "Data Controller", and "Original Disclosing Party", this addition creates an in-text contradiction, and the Receiving Party is left without a clear idea of to whom it owes a duty of notification.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1. Confidential Information For purposes of this Agreement, "Confidential Information" shall include all information or material that has or could have commercial value or other utility in the business in which Disclosing Party is engaged. If Confidential Information is in written form, the Disclosing Party shall label or stamp the materials with the word "Confidential" or some similar warning. If Confidential Information is transmitted orally, the Disclosing Party shall promptly provide writing indicating that such oral communication constitutes Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The change replaces "Disclosing Party" with "Data Fiduciary". The contradicted text explicitly states that the 'Disclosing Party' is responsible for labeling or stamping Confidential Information and providing written confirmation of oral communication constituting Confidential Information. By changing 'Disclosing Party' to 'Data Fiduciary' in the changed text, it creates an in-text contradiction, as it is now unclear to whom the Receiving Party should report breaches, contradicting the original intent of the contract, particularly in section 1 where the Disclosing Party has responsibilities.
